title: How Cats Survived the Black Death 🐱 The Hidden History
description: The medieval Black Death killed up to half of Europe. Cats had been blamed by the Pope and slaughtered for centuries. Discover the connection between cat purges, rat populations, and the spread of the plague — plus the science of why cats themselves resisted infection.

segments.voice: The Black Death wiped out an estimated 25 to 60% of Europe's population in just a few years. Cats had been actively hunted for nearly a century before that catastrophe. The question that historians keep asking is — did the persecution of cats help the plague spread faster than it should have?

Welcome to CatBrothers. The story starts in 1233 with Pope Gregory IX. He issued a papal bull called Vox in Rama linking black cats to satanic rituals. The document fueled an anti-cat panic that swept across Europe for the next two centuries. Cats were burned, killed in festivals, and viewed as demonic.

By the time the plague arrived in 1347, the European cat population had been hit hard in many regions. Without enough cats, rodent populations soared. Rats carried fleas. The fleas carried Yersinia pestis, the bacterium responsible for the plague. The chain was set, almost by accident.

Modern epidemiologists are split on how directly the cat decline affected plague spread. Some argue the connection is overstated. Others argue that even modest reductions in cat populations could have shifted the rodent balance enough to give the disease an opening. The data is still debated.

What's clearer is that cities with active cat populations fared somewhat better. London, Paris, and other major medieval centers experienced massive losses. Smaller towns where cats remained common appeared to suffer slightly less, though many factors muddy the data — including hygiene, trade routes, and climate.

Cats themselves can contract plague, but they don't spread it nearly as efficiently as rats. A 2000 paper in the Western Journal of Medicine documented occasional human cases linked to infected cats, but it required direct contact, and the transmission mode was different from rat-flea routes.

The bigger ecological role cats played was simple — they ate rats. A single domestic cat can kill dozens of rodents per year. Across an entire continent, that work was unmatched. No medieval pest control alternative came close to what a healthy cat population provided.

Once the plague began ravaging Europe, attitudes toward cats shifted. Slowly, populations recovered. Some historians point to the late 14th century as the moment when cats began regaining their reputation, especially in cities desperate to reduce rodent levels.

Witch hunts of the 16th and 17th centuries renewed cat persecution, but more locally and less systematically than the medieval purges. By that point, urban populations had largely accepted that cats were essential for keeping warehouses, granaries, and ships free of rats.

Sailors knew cats earned their keep. Ship cats became standard on European trading vessels. They hunted rats that destroyed cargo and spread disease. Some historians argue this naval tradition kept the cat species protected even during periods of land-based persecution. The sea offered safer harbor, literally.

Modern molecular research has examined the bacterium itself. Yersinia pestis was sequenced from medieval graves, and scientists at the Max Planck Institute confirmed the strain that caused the Black Death matches modern samples closely. The bacterium hasn't changed dramatically — but the conditions that let it spread certainly have.

Cats themselves have a degree of natural resistance to plague compared to rodents. Their immune systems handle Yersinia pestis better, and even when infected, they tend to clear the bacterium more effectively. That's part of why cats kept hunting plague-carrying rats without becoming a major spread vector themselves.

Historians like Donald Engels, who wrote Classical Cats in 1999, argue the role of cats in plague history has been underestimated. Engels traced the use of cats in pest control back to ancient Egypt and showed that wherever cats thrived, rodent-borne disease typically declined.

Egyptian society treated cats with deep reverence. They were associated with the goddess Bastet. Killing a cat was a serious offense, sometimes punished by death. That cultural protection contributed to large, healthy cat populations and reportedly to lower rates of grain-related rodent disease.

When that protection vanished in medieval Europe, the consequences played out over generations. The lesson — cultural attitudes toward animals can shape public health outcomes more than people realize. A single religious decree in 1233 may have rippled into one of history's worst pandemics.

There's another twist in the story. Recent research from McMaster University suggests human-to-human transmission via human fleas and lice may have played a larger role than previously believed. If accurate, that changes how much credit or blame cats deserve in the plague timeline.

But even with this revised model, rodent populations still acted as a reservoir for the bacterium. Cats reduce rodents. Reduce rodents and you reduce reservoirs. The chain still matters, even if the final delivery system to humans was more diverse than rats and rat fleas alone.

After the plague ended, Europe rebuilt. Cats once again became valued allies. Renaissance art shows cats appearing in domestic scenes, paintings, and literature with renewed warmth. By the 1500s, cats were no longer demonic figures in popular culture — they were companions and pest controllers.

The plague returned to Europe periodically for centuries. The Great Plague of London in 1665 killed about 100,000 people. Records from that period show ongoing cat protection in many neighborhoods, with cats actively used to suppress rats during outbreaks. The lesson was learned, painfully.

Cats continued earning their keep in cities, farms, and warehouses well into the 20th century. The introduction of modern rodenticides reduced their pest control role somewhat, but in countries like the United Kingdom and Japan, cats remained essential workers in agriculture for decades.

Today, plague still exists. Around 1,000 to 2,000 human cases are reported globally each year, mostly in rural Africa, Asia, and the western United States. Modern antibiotics treat it effectively. But the disease remains a reminder of how thin the line between civilization and catastrophe really is.

Veterinarians still occasionally diagnose plague in cats, especially in the American Southwest. Cats that hunt rodents in plague-endemic regions can contract the bacterium and pass it to humans. Pet owners in those areas are warned to keep cats indoors and apply flea control rigorously.

The medieval lesson echoes forward. Persecuting an entire species based on superstition can have unintended consequences that take centuries to fully reveal. Whether or not cat purges directly worsened the Black Death, the broader principle stands. Animals occupy ecological roles. Disrupt them, and humans pay.

So when you see your housecat ignore you on the couch, remember they descend from a lineage that survived medieval purges, plague, and human stupidity. They carry on a legacy of quiet, deadly competence at controlling rodents. Civilization owes them more than most people realize.
Subscribe to CatBrothers and hit the bell. What's coming next week — why your cat sits on every piece of paper you put down and the territorial science behind it. See you in the next documentary.
